questions.question: A darkroom maze (labyrinth) entrance or interlocked double-door vestibule exists so that:
options: Chemical fumes cannot escape into the plant
People can enter and leave without admitting white light to the room
The room stays at constant temperature
Loaded cassettes can be passed out without a hatch
questions.explanation: Entrance design is light control: overlapping maze walls or doors that cannot open together let traffic flow while unprocessed film stays protected from white light.

questions.question: The accepted way to check a darkroom for white-light leaks is to:
options: Shine a flashlight around the door from inside with the lights on
Process an unexposed film once a year and measure its density
Sit in the closed room for 10-15 minutes until dark-adapted, then inspect the door frame, fixtures, and penetrations for any visible glow
Check the room briefly each morning as you walk in
questions.explanation: Dark adaptation takes minutes; only adapted eyes can spot the faint leaks that film - which integrates light over its whole handling time - will record as fog.

questions.question: Mercury thermometers are prohibited in the darkroom because:
options: Mercury expands too slowly for accurate processing readings
A broken bulb contaminates the processing chemistry and creates a toxic hazard
Mercury glows faintly and can fog film
They cannot read temperatures below 68 °F
questions.explanation: One broken thermometer puts mercury into tanks, drains, and floor cracks - ruining chemistry and creating a hazardous cleanup - so alcohol, dial, or digital units are used instead.

questions.question: Jagged black tree- and branch-shaped marks on a processed radiograph are characteristic of:
options: Static discharge from sliding or rapidly pulling film in dry air
Inadequate fixing
Safelight fog through a cracked filter
Water spots from skipping the wetting agent
questions.explanation: Static electricity exposes the emulsion at the discharge points, printing lightning-like branches or dot clusters; slow deliberate handling and proper humidity prevent it.

questions.question: A box of film comes out of refrigerated storage for immediate use. The correct practice is to:
options: Open it at once - cold film performs best
Let the sealed package warm to room temperature before opening, so moisture cannot condense on the emulsion
Warm it quickly on top of the drying cabinet
Open it in the refrigerator so condensation forms outside the box
questions.explanation: Breaking the moisture-proof seal while the film is cold lets room moisture condense directly onto the cold emulsion; warming the unopened package first prevents it.

questions.question: For a week, every film from a manual line has come out low in density and flat in contrast despite proven exposure techniques. The most likely cause is:
options: Backscatter reaching the film
Exhausted, diluted, cold, or under-timed developer
Excessive fixing time
Safelight fog on the loading bench
questions.explanation: Weak development is the classic cause of uniformly thin, flat films across all work; fog from safelights or scatter would raise density, not lower it.

questions.question: A film shows a net-like, cracked pattern across the whole emulsion after manual processing. This reticulation was caused by:
options: A large temperature difference between successive processing solutions
Too much agitation in the developer
An over-strength wetting agent
Light leaking into the cassette during exposure
questions.explanation: Sudden temperature shock makes the gelatin swell and shrink unevenly into the characteristic network - the reason all baths are held within about 5 °F of the developer.

questions.question: Fresh fixer cleared your film type in 1.5 minutes. Today the same film takes 3 minutes to clear. You should:
options: Fix for at least 6 minutes and replenish or replace the fixer, since clearing time has doubled
Fix for 3 minutes exactly and carry on
Skip the stop bath to let the film clear faster
Raise the fixer temperature 10 °F to restore speed
questions.explanation: Minimum fixing is twice the clearing time - 6 minutes here - and a clearing time at double the fresh value is the standard signal the bath is exhausted.

questions.question: Typical operating figures for an industrial roller-transport processor are about:
options: 68 °F developer and one hour dry-to-dry
80 °F developer and 8-12 minutes dry-to-dry
120 °F developer and 90 seconds dry-to-dry
60 °F developer and 5 minutes dry-to-dry
questions.explanation: Processor chemistry runs hot - near 80 °F with hardened emulsions formulated for it - delivering a dry industrial radiograph in roughly 8-12 minutes; manual lines run 68 °F and an hour or more.

questions.question: This morning's sensitometric strip plots outside the speed action limit on the processor control chart. The correct response is to:
options: Note it on the chart and process production film normally
Hold production film, find and correct the cause, then rerun a strip that lands inside the limits before resuming
Adjust the densitometer until the reading falls inside the limits
Run two more strips and average the three readings
questions.explanation: An out-of-limit point means the processor is no longer the system your techniques were built on; production waits until the cause is corrected and a fresh strip proves recovery.
